summary: DHH reflects on how material possessions inevitably lose their novelty through the hedonic treadmill, while nature never stops inspiring awe. He shares his experience of watching sunrises from his Malibu home office and references research on forest bathing to argue that regular exposure to nature improves health and happiness. He connects this to his advocacy for remote work, suggesting that trading commute time for time in nature is one of its most underappreciated benefits.
key_points: Material possessions lose their wow factor over time due to the hedonic treadmill, but nature never does
Moving from urban Copenhagen to wilderness California reinforced that nature ranks higher than city life on his personal scorecard
Research on forest bathing supports the idea that walking in nature reduces stress and blood pressure more than urban walking
Remote work enables trading stressful commute time for restorative time in nature
money_quotes: I've found that you eventually get bored or at least used to every form of material trapping you can buy. Cars, watches, cameras, computers, whatever.
It's not that fancy stuff stops being nice, but it does stop being wow.
If you literally stop to smell the flowers, you'll never stop being wowed by the awesomeness of this planet.
I've seen a sunrise like this in Malibu a million times before, but it never, ever grows old.
Your life will literally be longer, healthier, and happier, if you let nature wash over you regularly.

Imagine taking that hour or two of commuting – which, when done in rush hour by car, is perhaps one of the most blood pressure and stress raising activities known to man! – and trading that in for a walk in the forest or the garden or any form of wilderness.
key_insight: The hedonic treadmill dulls every material purchase, but nature's capacity to inspire awe is inexhaustible — and remote work is the practical key to accessing it daily.
